question: In the appendiceal adenocarcinoma molecular classification, which two RAS-pathway genes co-drive the mucinous subtype?

agentic.answer:
I'll help you find information about the molecular classification of appendiceal adenocarcinoma and the RAS-pathway genes involved in the mucinous subtype.I found it! Let me read the paper on appendiceal adenocarcinoma molecular classification:Based on the [Molecular Classification of Appendiceal Adenocarcinoma](papers/36493333.html) study, the two RAS-pathway genes that co-drive the mucinous subtype are **[KRAS](genes/KRAS.html) and [NRAS](genes/NRAS.html)**. 

In the indolent RAS-mutant predominant subtype, mutations in KRAS or NRAS occur in the absence of [GNAS](genes/GNAS.html) and [TP53](genes/TP53.html) alterations, marking the clinically favorable molecular class with the best overall survival and lowest mutational burden.
agentic.answer_preview:
I'll help you find information about the molecular classification of appendiceal adenocarcinoma and the RAS-pathway genes involved in the mucinous subtype.I found it! Let me read the paper on appendiceal adenocarcinoma molecular classification:Based on the [Molecular Classification of Appendiceal Adenocarcinoma](papers/36493333.html) study, the two RAS-pathway genes that co-drive the mucinous subtype are **[KRAS](genes/KRAS.html) and [NRAS](genes/NRAS.html)**. 

In the indolent RAS-mutant predominant subtype, mutations in KRAS or NRAS occur in the absence of [GNAS](genes/GNAS.html) and [TP53](genes/TP53.html) alterations, marking the clinically favorable molecular class with the best overall survival and lowest mutational burden.

scores.reasoning: The answer incorrectly identifies NRAS as the co-driver with KRAS for the mucinous subtype, when the correct answer is KRAS and GNAS. While GNAS is mentioned in the answer, it is incorrectly described as being absent in the relevant subtype rather than being a co-driver of the mucinous subtype. The citation is correct.

rag.answer:
Based on the retrieved passages, **GNAS and RAS (specifically KRAS and NRAS)** are the two genes that co-drive the mucinous appendiceal adenocarcinoma (MAAP) subtype.

The study found that "GNAS and RAS mutations significantly (BH-corrected P = .006) co-occurred. Of 85 patients with GNAS-mutant (mut) tumors, 91% of these tumors (77/85) were also RAS-mut" (PMID:36493333). Additionally, KRAS was the most prevalent RAS mutation, found in 79% of MAAP tumors (130/164), compared to NRAS mutations in only 2% (PMID:36493333).

The study identified GNAS-mut predominant as one of the major molecular subtypes, defined as "GNAS-mut/RAS-wt or GNAS-mut/RAS-mut, all TP53-wt," highlighting the frequent co-occurrence of these two genes in driving mucinous appendiceal adenocarcinoma biology (PMID:36493333).
